questions.stem: You have just finished editing a JHotDraw source file and run 'git add -u'. What is the single most appropriate next command to permanently record this change?
questions.explanation: After 'add' moves changes into the index, 'commit' records them into the local repository (HEAD). 'push' shares already-committed work, 'clone' copies a remote, and 'fetch' downloads remote commits. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: You committed a fix on your feature branch yesterday, but a teammate who just pulled still cannot see it. What is the right next action?

options.text: Run 'git push' to send your commit to the remote
Re-clone the repository to refresh history
Run 'git commit' again to make the commit count
Ask the teammate to run 'git fetch' a second time

questions.explanation: A commit only records to your local HEAD; in a decentralized VCS teammates see nothing until you push. Re-committing, re-cloning, or a second fetch on their side cannot reveal a commit that never left your machine. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: You ran 'git fetch' and the commit log shows new commits, yet your working files have not changed. What should you do to bring those changes into your workspace?

options.text: Run 'git add -u' to stage the new files
Run 'git commit' to apply the fetched commits
Run 'git merge' to integrate the fetched commits
Run 'git clone' again to download them properly

questions.explanation: 'fetch' only downloads commits into the local repository; the workspace stays unchanged until you 'merge' (pull = fetch + merge). Staging, committing, or re-cloning do not integrate already-fetched commits. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: You want to grab your teammate's pushed changes and update your working files in a single command. Which is the best choice?
questions.explanation: 'pull' is fetch + merge in one step, so it both downloads and updates the workspace. 'fetch' alone leaves the workspace untouched, 'push' sends rather than receives, and 'commit' is local-only. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: A colleague insists 'commit and push are basically the same thing.' Which statement correctly identifies the key difference?

options.text: commit shares with the remote; push only records locally
both immediately publish changes to all teammates
commit records to your local repository; push shares those commits with the remote
commit stages files; push records them locally

questions.explanation: The defining difference is locality: commit records to your local HEAD, and only push transmits those commits to the remote. The other options invert the two, confuse commit with staging, or wrongly claim both publish. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: What is the essential difference between a centralized VCS like Subversion and a decentralized VCS like Git?

options.text: Centralized stores deltas; decentralized stores full copies
Centralized supports branching; decentralized forbids branching
Centralized clients hold no full history; decentralized clones each hold the complete history
Centralized works offline; decentralized requires a constant server connection

questions.explanation: The defining contrast is history location: a centralized working copy lacks full history, while every decentralized clone has the complete local repository. The other options invert offline capability or invent false limits on branching and storage. (Git Lab p.7)

questions.stem: You are told 'this tool keeps a single central repository that all developers talk to directly, and your checkout has no full history.' Which VCS type is being described?
questions.explanation: A single central repository plus a history-less working copy describes a centralized VCS (e.g. CVS, Subversion). Git and the decentralized/distributed types instead give every clone the complete history. (Git Lab p.7)

questions.stem: A version control system whose original purpose was managing the Linux kernel efficiently, created by Linus Torvalds, is which tool?
questions.explanation: Git is the decentralized VCS created by Linus Torvalds to manage large projects, originally the Linux kernel. Subversion and CVS are centralized, and Maven is a build tool, not a VCS. (Git Lab p.9)

questions.stem: Given the command 'git diff HEAD', what comparison is being requested?

options.text: The local repository against the remote
The workspace against the last commit (HEAD)
Two remote branches against each other

questions.explanation: 'diff HEAD' compares the workspace with the last commit. Plain 'diff' compares the workspace with the index/stage; the local-vs-remote and branch-vs-branch comparisons are not what this command does. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Reading the four-area model, which command's direction is described correctly?
questions.explanation: push moves commits from the local repository to the remote. commit goes index->local, clone goes remote->workspace, and add goes workspace->index, so the other three are scrambled. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: A developer runs: 'git add -u' then 'git commit -m "fix"' then stops for the day. The next morning a teammate cannot see the fix on the remote. Which area still holds the fix?

options.text: Only the index/stage; commit failed silently
Only the local repository (HEAD); it was never pushed to the remote
Only the remote; the teammate needs to re-clone
Only the workspace; commit does not move anything

questions.explanation: add staged it and commit moved it into the local repository (HEAD), but without a push the remote never received it, so teammates see nothing. The fix is past the index and is not on the remote or merely in the workspace. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which Git workflow order is correct when making and recording a change locally?

options.text: git diff -> git commit -> git add -u -> git status
git add -u -> git commit -> git status -> git diff
git status -> git diff -> git add -u -> git commit
git commit -> git status -> git diff -> git add -u

questions.explanation: You inspect first (status to see which files, diff to see what changed), then stage (add -u), then record (commit). Committing or staging before inspecting defeats the documented inspection loop. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the six listed benefits of version control?

options.text: Enforcing discipline through a defined workflow

questions.explanation: Compilation is a build-tool job, not a VCS benefit. Enforcing discipline, keeping history, and enabling collaboration are three of the six benefits the Git Lab lists. (Git Lab p.3)

questions.stem: All of the following belong under version control EXCEPT which one?
questions.explanation: The 'Don't do it' slide warns against committing large binaries and executables; commit source only. Source files, pom.xml, and plain-text config are all legitimate source artifacts. (Git Lab p.6)

questions.stem: During the JHotDraw Maven build you notice the 'target/' directory was committed. Why is this a mistake?

options.text: target/ is encrypted and cannot be tracked by Git
It is generated build output, not source; only source belongs in version control
Committing target/ deletes the src/ directory
Maven refuses to run if target/ is under version control

questions.explanation: target/ holds generated build output, and the rule is to commit source, not build output, because generated artifacts bloat history and conflict. It is not encrypted, does not delete src/, and Maven still runs. (Git Lab p.6)

questions.stem: Reading the area model, you see four boxes: 'working dir', 'stage', 'HEAD', and 'origin'. Which box is the staging area for the next commit?
questions.explanation: 'stage' is the index, the staging area of changes selected for the next commit. The working dir holds disk files, HEAD is committed local history, and origin is the remote. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: You want a complete local copy of a remote repository, linked to it as 'origin'. Which command fits best?
questions.explanation: clone creates a complete local copy of the remote and links it as origin. fetch only downloads commits into an existing repo, add stages files, and commit records locally. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: Two teammates each commit different fixes to their own local repositories and neither pushes. What is true of the remote (origin)?

options.text: It auto-merges both fixes for them
It receives both fixes automatically on commit
It locks until one developer reverts
It has neither fix until someone pushes

questions.explanation: Because commit is local in a decentralized VCS, the remote receives nothing until someone pushes; it does not auto-receive on commit, auto-merge, or lock. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Given the description 'compares your edited files on disk against what is in the index', which command is being described?
questions.explanation: Plain 'git diff' compares the workspace against the index/stage. 'diff HEAD' compares against the last commit, 'show' displays the last commit, and 'status' only lists which files changed. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Given the description 'displays the most recent commit', which command does the local-repo slide use?
questions.explanation: 'git show' displays the last commit. 'status' lists changed files, 'diff' shows uncommitted changes, and 'fetch' downloads from the remote. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: You skipped running 'git status' and 'git diff' and committed straight away, then discovered you recorded an unrelated file. Which documented practice would have prevented this?

options.text: Always inspect with status then diff before committing
Always run the Maven build before committing

questions.explanation: The flagged pitfall is skipping the inspection loop; running status then diff first tells you exactly what you are about to record. Pushing, re-cloning, or building first does not reveal what is staged. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the three essential VCS actions?
questions.explanation: The three essential actions are Add, Commit, and Update; Branch is a real Git operation but not among the three named essentials. (Git Lab p.4)

questions.stem: In the generic two-developer VCS workflow on foo.txt, which best describes the role of the central repository?

options.text: The single synchronisation point each developer commits to and updates from
A backup that developers cannot read from, only write to
A private vault that hides each developer's copy from the other
A compiler that turns foo.txt into an executable on commit

questions.explanation: The central repository is the single synchronisation point: each developer add+commits up to it and updates to receive the other's changes. It does not hide copies, compile, or block reads. (Git Lab p.5)

questions.stem: A teammate claims 'pull just magically grabs everything in one atomic step that fetch can't do.' What is the more accurate description?

options.text: pull re-clones the repository from scratch each time
pull is simply fetch followed by merge
pull pushes your commits and then downloads theirs
pull bypasses the local repository entirely

questions.explanation: pull is nothing more than fetch plus merge in one step. It does not bypass the local repo, push your commits, or re-clone. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the four areas in the Git four-area model?
questions.explanation: The four areas are workspace, index/stage, local repository (HEAD), and remote repository. 'Backlog' belongs to the agile loop, not the Git model. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: You need to stage already-tracked modified files for the next commit. Which command and flag fit best?
questions.explanation: 'add -u' stages already-tracked modified files (workspace -> index). 'commit -a' records rather than stages, and there is no '-u' meaning here on push or fetch. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which statement about a decentralized VCS is FALSE?

options.text: Repositories can synchronise via a shared remote
Every clone holds a complete local repository
Git is an example of this type
Only the central server retains the project history

questions.explanation: It is false that only the server keeps history; in a decentralized VCS every clone holds the full history. The other statements correctly describe Git's decentralized model. (Git Lab p.7)

questions.stem: Which valued benefit of version control is best described by 'who changed what, when, and why'?
questions.explanation: Maintaining historical information is the who/what/when/why captured in commit messages. Disk space is about deltas, recovery is about reversibility, and discipline is about the commit/update workflow. (Git Lab p.3)

questions.stem: Why does the guide call the commit-vs-push distinction the single most exam-relevant Git concept?

options.text: It controls whether Maven skips tests
It determines which branch HEAD points to
It is what lets Git store versions as deltas
It is the defining behaviour of a decentralized VCS: commit is local, push shares

questions.explanation: commit records only to local HEAD and push is what shares it, which is the defining feature of a decentralized VCS and a classic exam trap. It is unrelated to delta storage, Maven, or HEAD's branch. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which command receives changes from the server in the remote-workflow slide?
questions.explanation: git pull receives changes from the server. push sends them, add stages, and status lists changed files. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: Which URL scheme is NOT among those the remote-workflow slide says a clone repository argument can use?
questions.explanation: The slide lists file, http, and ssh as valid clone schemes; smtp is a mail protocol and is not used to clone a repository. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: Read this session: 'git clone <url>' then 'git add -u' then 'git commit -m "x"' then 'git push'. Which transition does the LAST command perform?
questions.explanation: push moves commits from the local repository to the remote. add did workspace->index, commit did index->local, and clone did remote->workspace, so only push remains as local->remote. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Your teammate pushed a branch to origin and asks you to integrate it. Which single command both downloads and merges their work into your files?
questions.explanation: git pull = fetch + merge, so it downloads and integrates in one step. fetch only downloads, clone makes a fresh copy, and add stages local changes. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which pair correctly lists examples of centralized version control systems?
questions.explanation: CVS and Subversion are centralized. Git and Mercurial are decentralized, GitHub/GitLab are hosting platforms, and Maven/Gradle are build tools. (Git Lab p.7)

questions.stem: Why is GitHub NOT the same thing as Git?

options.text: GitHub is a hosting platform for remote repositories; Git is the VCS itself
GitHub is a centralized VCS while Git is decentralized
GitHub stores deltas while Git stores full copies
GitHub is a build system while Git is a VCS

questions.explanation: GitHub is introduced as a hosting platform for remote repositories, whereas Git is the decentralized VCS. GitHub is not itself a VCS type or a build system. (Git Lab p.16)

questions.stem: A student writes 'git commit conserves disk space because it shares deltas with the team.' Which two confusions does this single sentence contain?

options.text: commit does share with the team, and deltas are irrelevant to Git
commit stages files, and the team always sees staged files
commit does not share with the team (push does), and delta storage is a VCS storage property, not what commit 'does'
commit re-clones the repo, and disk space is unaffected by versions

questions.explanation: commit records only locally (push is what shares), and conserving disk space via deltas is a general VCS benefit, not the effect of committing. The other options misstate Git further. (Git Lab p.3, p.11)

questions.stem: After 'git clone', what default name links your local repository to the server it came from?
questions.explanation: clone links to the remote named origin by default. upstream is a convention for a second remote, main is a branch name, and HEAD is the current-commit pointer. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: Which everyday sequence does the 'Local Repositories' slide present as an example workflow?

options.text: git commit -> edit -> git status -> git push
Edit -> git status -> git diff -> git commit -a
git clone -> git push -> git status -> edit
git push -> edit -> git diff -> git commit

questions.explanation: The example loop is: do some editing, git status to see changed files, git diff to see changes, then git commit -a to record. The other orderings put recording or pushing before inspection. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: Which command would you use to send your local commits to the server?
questions.explanation: git push sends your local commits to the server. pull and fetch receive changes, and status only lists what changed. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: From the disk outward to the server, which ordering of Git areas is correct?

options.text: remote repository -> local repository -> index/stage -> workspace
workspace -> index/stage -> local repository -> remote repository
index/stage -> workspace -> remote repository -> local repository
workspace -> local repository -> index/stage -> remote repository

questions.explanation: From disk outward the order is workspace -> index/stage -> local repository (HEAD) -> remote. The other options shuffle these areas. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: A teammate says 'I'll just fetch and my files will update automatically, no merge needed, because Git is decentralized.' What is the flaw in this reasoning?

options.text: fetch updates the workspace but not the local repository
fetch is impossible in a decentralized VCS
fetch only updates the local repository; the workspace needs a merge regardless of decentralization
decentralization means files update only after a push

questions.explanation: fetch downloads commits into the local repository but leaves the workspace unchanged until you merge; decentralization does not change that. fetch is fully supported, and it updates the repo, not the workspace. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which Git command does NOT touch the remote repository in any way?
questions.explanation: commit records only to the local repository and never contacts the remote. push, fetch, and pull all communicate with the remote. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: You want to verify exactly what your single last commit contained before sending it to teammates. Which command is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: git show displays the most recent commit, letting you review what it contained. status lists uncommitted changes, diff shows uncommitted differences, and clone copies a remote. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: In the essential VCS actions, which one means 'pull the latest state from the repository'?
questions.explanation: Update pulls the latest state from the repository. Add stages, Commit records, and Clone is not one of the three essential actions. (Git Lab p.4)

questions.stem: Before touching any JHotDraw code, your team logs the requested change as a GitHub Issue. Which canonical change-process phase does this map onto?
questions.explanation: Filing a change request as a GitHub Issue is the course's first instance of Initiation: a request enters the system before any code is touched. Actualization, Verification, and Impact Analysis are later phases. (Git Lab p.19)

questions.stem: How does Lecture 1 relate to the canonical change process taught in later lectures?

options.text: It replaces the change process with the agile loop
It names Brooks as the author of the change process
It walks through Concept Location to Verification in full detail
It sets up the course and implies the process via the portfolio brief, but does not walk through the phases

questions.explanation: Lecture 1 sets up the course and only implies the process through the portfolio brief; the canonical phases are named later. It does not detail them, replace them, or attribute them to Brooks. (Introduction p.6)

questions.stem: Which ordering of canonical change-process phases does the guide say Lecture 1 foreshadows for portfolio changes?

options.text: Actualization -> Verification -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis
Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Verification
Impact Analysis -> Concept Location -> Verification -> Prefactoring

questions.explanation: The guide lists Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Verification. The other orderings scramble these phases. (Introduction p.6)

questions.stem: Filing a GitHub Issue is presented as conceptually important for what reason beyond Git mechanics?

options.text: It replaces Impact Analysis so no dependency check is needed
It is identical to pushing a feature branch
It is a change request entering the system before any code is changed
It is the final Verification recorded after code is complete

questions.explanation: The Issue is the first Initiation step: a change request enters the system before code is touched. It is not Verification, does not replace Impact Analysis, and is not the same as a push. (Git Lab p.19)

questions.stem: Which three threads does the lecture say it connects from day one and revisits all semester?

options.text: A greenfield app, a fixed specification, and centralized SVN
A database schema, a UML diagram, and a release tag
A legacy codebase (JHotDraw), a disciplined change workflow, and version control
A compiler, a waterfall plan, and manual file copying

questions.explanation: From day one the course links JHotDraw (legacy codebase), the disciplined change workflow, and version control as the substrate for traceable change. The other options describe unrelated artifacts. (Introduction p.6)

questions.stem: A student claims a maintenance course is unnecessary because good design avoids ever changing code. Which fact best refutes that claim?

options.text: It removes the need to understand existing code
It reverses code decay so software can stay in the Evolution stage
It replaces version control with a central repository
It speeds the close-down of legacy systems

questions.explanation: Reengineering/refactoring fights code decay and keeps software in the Evolution stage, which is exactly what a maintenance course teaches. It does not accelerate retirement, replace VCS, or eliminate the need to understand code. (Introduction p.22)

questions.stem: Which property is the odd one out because it is accidental rather than an essential difficulty?
questions.explanation: The chosen OS is an accidental property (replaceable technology), unlike the essential difficulties complexity, invisibility, and conformity. (Introduction p.9-10)

questions.stem: A manager says 'once we migrate to the latest framework, our software will stop being so hard to change.' Why does the lecture say this hope is misplaced?

options.text: Changeability only appears during the servicing stage
The latest framework removes invisibility but not changeability
Frameworks are essential difficulties, not accidental ones
Changeability is an essential difficulty that persists regardless of the framework

questions.explanation: Changeability is an essential difficulty that no accidental tool/framework choice removes; only accidental properties change with the framework. It is not stage-specific, and frameworks are accidental, not essential. (Introduction p.9-10)

questions.stem: Given the description 'software has no natural geometric form, so you cannot see it like a bridge', which essential difficulty is meant?
questions.explanation: Invisibility is the lack of a natural visual/geometric form, which is why we rely on visualization tools. Complexity is about parts, conformity about external standards, and discontinuity about disproportionate behaviour. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Given the description 'a small input change triggers a disproportionately large, non-continuous change in behaviour, so the software does not degrade gracefully', which difficulty is described?
questions.explanation: Discontinuity captures that software does not degrade gracefully; small inputs can cause large behavioural jumps. Changeability is pressure to change, complexity is about parts, and conformity is external constraints. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the five essential difficulties?
questions.explanation: Portability is not among the five essential difficulties (complexity, invisibility, changeability, conformity, discontinuity); it is closer to an accidental, technology-bound concern. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: A student writes: 'Complexity and the chosen platform are both accidental, so both will fade as tooling improves.' What is wrong with this claim?

options.text: Complexity is essential and will not fade; only the platform is accidental
Complexity is accidental but the platform is essential
Both are essential and neither fades
Neither is a property the lecture discusses

questions.explanation: Complexity is an essential difficulty that tooling cannot remove, while the platform is accidental and does change. The claim wrongly labels complexity accidental. (Introduction p.9-10)

questions.stem: A wall of brand logos (WordPress, .NET, AJAX, iPhone, Android, etc.) on a slide is illustrating which idea?
questions.explanation: The logo wall illustrates accidental properties, the replaceable technologies/platforms of an era. It is not the essential difficulties, the Git areas, or the agile loop. (Introduction p.10)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the three software-engineering paradigms?
questions.explanation: The V-model is a life-span model, not one of the three paradigms (ad hoc, waterfall, agile/iterative). (Introduction p.12)

questions.stem: Which characterization best distinguishes the ad hoc paradigm from waterfall?

options.text: Ad hoc is a repeating loop; waterfall is craft without process
Ad hoc is craft without a defined process; waterfall imposes a strict linear sequence of phases
Ad hoc pairs design with testing; waterfall ships versions in parallel
Ad hoc fixes requirements up front; waterfall has no requirements

questions.explanation: Ad hoc is craft without process, while waterfall is a strict linear sequence borrowed from construction. The other options describe agile, the V-model, or invert the two. (Introduction p.13-15)

questions.stem: Why does waterfall, despite its intuitive appeal, struggle on multi-month IT projects?

options.text: It requires a decentralized VCS that did not yet exist
It forbids any testing until the very end
It cannot be drawn as a diagram
Its fixed up-front spec goes obsolete as requirements change ~2-3% per month

questions.explanation: Waterfall assumes requirements are fixed up front, but they change ~2-3%/month, so the spec is obsolete before delivery. The other options are not the deck's stated reason. (Introduction p.15-16)

questions.stem: Which two empirical anomalies does the deck use to undermine waterfall's promise of predictability?

options.text: Requirements volatility (~2-3%/month) and CHAOS challenged/failed rates

questions.explanation: The two anomalies are requirements volatility (Capers Jones) and the Standish CHAOS data showing many projects challenged or failed. The other pairs belong to Git or the staged model. (Introduction p.16-17)

questions.stem: In the per-software-type table, which type changes fastest, and at what monthly rate?
questions.explanation: Commercial software changes fastest at 3.5%/month. System and military are 2.0%, information systems 1.5%, and contract/outsource 1.0%. (Introduction p.17)

questions.stem: Which statement about the CHAOS Reports is FALSE?

options.text: Their success criterion is on time, on budget, satisfactory result
They define success as zero post-release defects
They are published by the Standish Group

questions.explanation: It is false that CHAOS defines success as zero defects; success is on time, on budget, with a satisfactory result. The other three statements are correct. (Introduction p.17)

questions.stem: On large projects, the CHAOS table shows agile far ahead of waterfall on success. Which figures are correct?
questions.explanation: For large projects, agile is 18% successful versus waterfall's 3% - the most dramatic gap. 39% vs 11% is all sizes, 58% vs 44% is small, and 27% vs 7% is medium. (Introduction p.19)

questions.stem: Despite agile beating waterfall in every CHAOS row, what conclusion does the summary insist on?

options.text: Ad hoc is now the only paradigm used in maintenance
Agile fully replaced ad hoc and waterfall
The three paradigms co-exist; none erased the others
Waterfall is the safest choice for large projects

questions.explanation: The summary stresses that ad hoc, waterfall, and agile co-exist, each surviving in suitable niches; none simply won. The other options claim a single paradigm replaced the rest. (Introduction p.27)

questions.stem: A student reads 'the paradigms co-exist' as 'every project must use all three at once.' Why is that misreading wrong?

options.text: Co-existence means waterfall is banned everywhere
Co-existence means they merged into one modern process
Co-existence means each paradigm still fits some contexts, not that all are combined per project

questions.explanation: Co-existence means each paradigm survives in suitable niches, not that they are merged or used together on every project, nor that one is banned. (Introduction p.27)

questions.stem: In the illustrated agile loop, what flows back into the backlog to drive the next iteration?
questions.explanation: Feedback from each release flows back into the backlog of items. A frozen spec is waterfall, the V-model is a life-span model, and cloning is a Git action. (Introduction p.18)

questions.stem: Which repeating cycle characterizes the agile paradigm rather than a single linear pass?
questions.explanation: Agile uses a repeating Plan -> Collaborate -> Deliver loop. The other triples are invented or mix in Git actions. (Introduction p.18)

questions.stem: Which relationship does the guide draw between the agile loop and the canonical change process?

options.text: The iterative change loop is the spiritual ancestor of the canonical change process
The agile loop replaces the canonical change process
They are unrelated and never connected
The canonical change process predates and contradicts agile

questions.explanation: The guide treats the iterative (agile) change loop as the spiritual ancestor of the canonical change process. It does not replace, contradict, or stand apart from it. (Introduction p.19)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the software life-span models presented in the lecture?
questions.explanation: Waterfall is a paradigm, not a life-span model. Staged, V-model, prototype (plus reengineering and versioned-staged) are the life-span models. (Introduction p.28)

questions.stem: What is the correct stage order in the staged life-span model?

options.text: Initial development -> Servicing -> Evolution -> Phase-out -> Close-down
Initial development -> Evolution -> Servicing -> Phase-out -> Close-down
Initial development -> Evolution -> Phase-out -> Servicing -> Close-down
Evolution -> Initial development -> Servicing -> Close-down -> Phase-out

questions.explanation: The order is Initial development -> Evolution -> Servicing -> Phase-out -> Close-down. The other options swap evolution/servicing or phase-out/servicing. (Introduction p.21)

questions.stem: What is the key difference between the Evolution stage and the Servicing stage in the staged model?

options.text: Evolution comes after phase-out; Servicing comes before initial development
Evolution makes substantial changes while the team understands the code; Servicing applies only small patches
Evolution handles binaries; Servicing handles source
Evolution applies only small patches; Servicing makes substantial changes

questions.explanation: Evolution is substantial change while the team still understands the code; once evolution stops, servicing applies only small patches. The other options invert or misplace the stages. (Introduction p.21)

questions.stem: What force naturally pushes software from Evolution toward Servicing, and what counter-force reverses it?

options.text: Forking pushes it down; merging pushes it back
Phase-out pushes it down; close-down pushes it back
Reengineering pushes it down; code decay pushes it back
Code decay pushes it down; reengineering pushes it back

questions.explanation: Code decay drives Evolution -> Servicing, and reengineering is the counter-force restoring decayed code (Servicing -> Evolution). The other pairs are Git operations or later stages. (Introduction p.22)

questions.stem: Given the description 'the arrow back from Servicing to Evolution that restores decayed code so deep change can resume', which transition is meant?
questions.explanation: Reengineering is the Servicing -> Evolution counter-force that restores decayed code. Code decay is the opposite direction, switch-off is Phase-out -> Close-down, and the first running version is Initial -> Evolution. (Introduction p.22)

questions.stem: Compared with the plain staged model, what extra capability does the versioned staged model add?

options.text: It removes the servicing stage entirely
It replaces stages with Git branches
It runs the staged life cycle in parallel across multiple shipped versions
It merges all versions into one trunk before evolution

questions.explanation: The versioned staged model runs the staged cycle in parallel across multiple shipped versions, each with its own life cycle. It keeps servicing, does not merge versions, and is not a Git-branch model. (Introduction p.23)

questions.stem: A product team must keep shipping release N while preparing release N+1. In the versioned staged model, what timing does this lead to for the next evolution branch?

options.text: Before the old version is closed down
Only after the old version reaches close-down
Only during the old version's phase-out

questions.explanation: A new evolution branch starts before the old one is closed down, modelling products that support several releases at once. The other options describe strictly sequential versioning, which the model rejects. (Introduction p.23)

questions.stem: Which idea is the defining feature of the V-model, distinguishing it from the other life-span models?

options.text: Each version ships in parallel with others
Each commit is shared immediately on push
Each constructive activity is paired with a corresponding verification activity
Each stage decays into the next

questions.explanation: The V-model pairs each constructive (descending) activity with a matching verification (ascending) activity. Parallel versions describe the versioned-staged model, decay belongs to reengineering, and commit/push is Git. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: Which V-model design-to-test pairing is INCORRECT?
questions.explanation: Requirements pairs with functional testing, not unit testing. The correct pairings are requirements<->functional, system design<->system, and unit design<->unit testing. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: On the V-model, whereabouts does maintenance fall, and what does that location tell you about when it happens?

options.text: At the top, above functional testing, as the post-delivery stage
Outside the model, as an optional add-on
At the bottom, as the first implementation step
On the descending arm, paired with unit design

questions.explanation: Maintenance sits at the top of the V above functional testing, as the post-delivery stage. Implementation is at the bottom, the descending arm is design, and maintenance is not optional. (Introduction p.24)

questions.stem: How do the staged model's Servicing stage and the V-model's maintenance node differ, despite both being post-development?

options.text: Both denote the very first stage before implementation
Servicing is the reduced patch-only stage after evolution; V-model maintenance is the top-of-V post-delivery stage
They are two names for the same initial-development stage
Both sit at the bottom of the V

questions.explanation: Servicing is the reduced patch-only stage after evolution stops, while V-model maintenance is placed at the top as the post-delivery stage. They are neither first stages, identical, nor at the bottom of the V. (Introduction p.21-24)

questions.stem: Your team builds an early throwaway version purely to learn what users actually need before committing to a design. In the prototype life-span model, what is this artifact's purpose?

options.text: To serve as the shipped final product
To act as the team's central version repository
To surface misunderstandings so corrected requirements drive the real build

questions.explanation: The prototype is an early throwaway/learning artifact that surfaces misunderstandings so corrected requirements drive the real build. It is not the shipped product, a repository, or a maintenance replacement. (Introduction p.25)

questions.stem: Which step comes immediately AFTER the prototype in the prototype model's sequence?
questions.explanation: The sequence is requirements -> prototype -> corrected requirements -> design -> implementation -> maintenance, so corrected requirements follow the prototype before design. (Introduction p.25)

questions.stem: A life-span model is described as building one throwaway artifact early so requirements can be fixed before design starts. Which model is being described?
questions.explanation: The prototype model inserts an early throwaway artifact to correct requirements before design. The staged model sequences stages, the V-model pairs design with testing, and the versioned model runs versions in parallel. (Introduction p.25)

questions.stem: A student attributes the five essential difficulties directly to a citation on the slides. What does the grounding note caution?

options.text: The ideas come from Brooks's 'No Silver Bullet', but the slides never name Brooks
The difficulties were invented by the Standish Group
The slides explicitly cite Brooks as the source
The difficulties come from the CHAOS Reports

questions.explanation: The five essential difficulties are Brooks's, but the slides never name him, so do not claim the deck cites Brooks. They are not from Standish or the CHAOS Reports. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: When the deck says software 'became a product in its own right', from what was it separated?
questions.explanation: Software was 'separated from the hardware' in the 1950s, becoming its own product and field. It was not framed as separated from the OS, network, or UI. (Introduction p.11)

questions.stem: If software is pervasive and long-lived, which economic conclusion about where effort goes follows most directly?

options.text: Most effort goes into greenfield development
Hardware costs now dominate software budgets
Most software effort goes into changing, fixing, and evolving existing systems

questions.explanation: Because software is pervasive and long-lived, the vast majority of effort goes into maintaining existing systems, justifying a maintenance course. The other options contradict this premise. (Introduction p.8)

questions.stem: Mapping onto JHotDraw: which staged-model stage best describes the portfolio work students do?
questions.explanation: Portfolio work on JHotDraw is evolution-style change - real change while the team understands the code - not mere servicing patches, phase-out, or close-down. (Introduction p.21)

questions.stem: What kind of software is JHotDraw, the recurring course case study?
questions.explanation: JHotDraw is a Java drawing-editor framework and a teaching vehicle for design patterns. It is not a VCS, CI server, or database. (Git Lab p.18)

questions.stem: Your team needs its own working copy of JHotDraw to make changes on GitHub. What is the prescribed first step?

options.text: Clone the original directly without forking
Mirror it to a centralized SVN server
Ask the instructor to email a zip of the source
Fork the project repository on GitHub

questions.explanation: Each team first forks the JHotDraw repository on GitHub, then members clone the fork and follow GitHub flow. There is no SVN mirror, emailed zip, or direct-original clone in the lab. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: After forking, which collaboration model do JHotDraw team members follow?

options.text: GitHub flow, each on their own feature branch
A single shared branch everyone commits to

questions.explanation: Members follow GitHub flow, each on their own feature branch so collaboration never collides. A single shared branch, lock-based check-in, and branchless trunk development are not prescribed. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: Which ordered sequence matches the IntroLab's intended bootstrap of JHotDraw?

options.text: Clone -> fork -> run svg Main -> mvn clean install
mvn clean install -> fork -> clone -> run svg Main
Fork on GitHub -> clone -> mvn clean install -DskipTests -> run svg Main
Run svg Main -> clone -> fork -> mvn clean install

questions.explanation: The intended order is fork on GitHub, clone the fork, build with mvn clean install -DskipTests, then run org.jhotdraw.samples.svg.Main. The other orderings build or run before the repo exists locally. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: You are setting up the IntroLab JHotDraw build on a clean machine. Which Maven-and-JDK pairing must you install to match the lab?
questions.explanation: The IntroLab specifies Maven 3.8.x on JDK 11. The other combinations use the wrong Maven/JDK versions or substitute Gradle. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: Adding '-DskipTests' to the IntroLab's first JHotDraw build achieves which outcome?

options.text: To permanently disable testing for the whole course
To skip downloading dependencies and build from cache
To run only the tests while skipping compilation
To get a fast smoke build confirming compilation without running the test suite

questions.explanation: -DskipTests gives a fast smoke build that confirms the modules compile and install without running tests. It does not permanently disable tests, invert into tests-only, or skip dependency download. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: You ran 'mvn clean install -DskipTests' successfully but the GUI does not open. Which exact main class must you target with mvn exec:java?
questions.explanation: The GUI launches with mvn exec:java targeting org.jhotdraw.samples.svg.Main; using the wrong class means the GUI will not appear (a flagged pitfall). The other class names are plausible but wrong. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: You have built JHotDraw and now want to launch the SVG sample editor. From which module folder does the IntroLab tell you to run the exec:java command?
questions.explanation: The GUI is run from jhotdraw-samples-misc. jhotdraw-core, jhotdraw-app, and jhotdraw-svg-main are not the folder named for launching the sample editor. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: What appears on screen after the JHotDraw exec:java command runs successfully?

options.text: The JHotDraw SVG sample editor GUI

questions.explanation: On success the JHotDraw SVG sample editor GUI appears, confirming a working environment. It is not a REPL, web server, or PDF. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: After committing on your feature branch, how do JHotDraw teammates get each other's work?

options.text: The instructor copies files between machines
One developer pushes the branch; teammates pull (fetch + merge)
Each developer re-clones the repository from scratch
They email patches and apply them by hand

questions.explanation: You push your branch to origin and teammates pull (= fetch + merge) to integrate. Re-cloning, emailing patches, and manual copying are not the prescribed mechanism. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which fixed technical environment does the day-one JHotDraw setup establish for the entire course?
questions.explanation: Day-one setup fixes JHotDraw + Maven + Git/GitHub as the environment for the whole course. The other combinations swap in the wrong project, build tool, or VCS. (IntroLab p.1)

questions.stem: Working through the LAB TODOs, a student has cloned JHotDraw and learned Git. As their first taste of course Initiation, what are they directed to create on GitHub?

options.text: A change request as an Issue

questions.explanation: LAB TODOs tell students to file a change request as an Issue - the first taste of course Initiation. It is not a pull request, release tag, or wiki page. (Git Lab p.19)

questions.stem: The SB5-MAI portfolio is graded on applying Clean Code to an existing editor's features rather than building anything new. Which task does that describe?

options.text: Port JHotDraw from Java to Kotlin
Write a formal specification for JHotDraw
Build a new drawing editor from scratch
Refactor JHotDraw features according to Clean Code

questions.explanation: The portfolio task is to refactor JHotDraw features according to Clean Code. It is refactoring of existing code, not greenfield construction, a language port, or a formal spec. (Introduction p.6)

questions.stem: On which scale is the SB5-MAI individual portfolio assessed?
questions.explanation: The portfolio is assessed on the Danish 7-point grading scale. It is graded rather than pass/fail and is not reported on a percentage or ECTS A-F scale. (Introduction p.5)

questions.stem: Which prerequisite size is paired correctly with its subject for SB5-MAI?
questions.explanation: Software engineering is ~20 ECTS, while advanced OO programming is ~12.5 ECTS and this course itself is ~5 ECTS. The other options misassign these sizes. (Introduction p.4)

questions.stem: Because of its heavy OO and software-engineering prerequisites, what does SB5-MAI assume you can already do?

options.text: Write OO software and design systems

questions.explanation: The prerequisites mean the course assumes you can already write OO software and design systems, so it can focus on changing software you did not write. Cluster ops, compiler authoring, and running surveys are not assumed. (Introduction p.4)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly maps a Git command to its four-area transition?

options.text: fetch: remote repository -> local repository (no merge)

questions.explanation: fetch downloads from the remote into the local repository without merging. pull (remote->workspace) is fetch+merge, merge integrates local->workspace, and push is local->remote, so the other options are wrong. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which command moves fetched commits from the local repository into your working files?
questions.explanation: merge integrates fetched/other commits from the local repository into the workspace. fetch only downloads, add stages, and push uploads to the remote. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: A developer chains: 'git status' (clean), then 'git diff' (empty), then 'git commit -a'. Why does the commit have nothing to record?

options.text: status and diff showed no changes, so there is nothing staged or modified to commit
commit -a only works after a push
diff deletes pending changes before commit

questions.explanation: A clean status and empty diff mean no files changed, so commit has nothing to record. diff and status are read-only inspections that do not delete or discard anything, and commit does not depend on a prior push. (Git Lab p.13)

questions.stem: Given the description 'must adapt to arbitrary external interfaces, standards, and institutions it cannot redesign', which essential difficulty is meant?
questions.explanation: Conformity is the need to conform to arbitrary external interfaces and institutions. Changeability is internal pressure to change, complexity is about parts, and invisibility is about visual form. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: On the Essential Properties slide, which difficulty is depicted with a padlock/security icon?
questions.explanation: Discontinuity is illustrated with a padlock/security icon, evoking that small changes cause large behavioural jumps. Complexity uses a polygon web, changeability uses arrows, and conformity uses modules M and D. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: Which essential difficulty is illustrated with branching/merging arrows, evoking version-control branches?
questions.explanation: Changeability is illustrated with branching/merging arrows, capturing constant pressure to change. Invisibility, conformity, and discontinuity use other illustrations. (Introduction p.9)

questions.stem: An exam answer claims 'a faster CI server will remove our software's invisibility problem.' Why is that wrong?

options.text: Invisibility is accidental, fixed by faster tooling
Invisibility is an essential difficulty; a CI server is accidental tooling that cannot remove it
CI servers convert software into a visual form

questions.explanation: Invisibility is an essential difficulty that accidental tooling like a CI server cannot remove; only visualization helps cope, not eliminate it. It is not accidental and not stage-specific. (Introduction p.9-10)

questions.stem: Two slogans on the motivation slide argue that software pervades modern life. Which pair are they?
questions.explanation: The motivation slides use 'Internet in everything' and 'Software is everywhere'. The other slogans are popular tech phrases not in the deck. (Introduction p.8)

questions.stem: Waterfall is called 'an intuitively appealing metaphor' precisely because it mimics a linear process from another field. Which industries is that metaphor borrowed from?
questions.explanation: Waterfall is a linear process borrowed from construction and manufacturing, an intuitively appealing metaphor. The other industries are not its source. (Introduction p.15)

questions.stem: Histories of the ad hoc era note the first programmers were drawn from two technical communities. Which two?
questions.explanation: In the ad hoc era programmers came from the ranks of hardware engineers and mathematicians. The other professions are plausible-sounding but not stated. (Introduction p.14)

questions.stem: Roughly at what monthly rate does the deck say IT requirements change overall?
questions.explanation: Requirements for IT change at 2 to 3 % per month (Capers Jones). The per-type figures range from 1.0% to 3.5% per month, but the headline overall rate is 2-3%/month. (Introduction p.16)

questions.stem: On a multi-month project where requirements drift a few percent each month, what failure does a frozen up-front specification run into?

options.text: At 2-3%/month, the spec is obsolete before delivery
Maven cannot build against a specification

questions.explanation: At 2-3% change per month, a multi-month project's fixed spec is obsolete before delivery - waterfall's core problem. The other options are irrelevant to the volatility argument. (Introduction p.16)

questions.stem: Which of these does NOT belong with the staged model's named stages?
questions.explanation: Prototype is a separate life-span model, not a stage of the staged model (Initial development, Evolution, Servicing, Phase-out, Close-down). (Introduction p.21)

questions.stem: Which transition label and its staged-model direction are paired CORRECTLY?
questions.explanation: Code decay is the Evolution -> Servicing drift. Reengineering goes the opposite way, switch-off is Phase-out -> Close-down, and servicing patches are a Servicing loop. (Introduction p.22)

questions.stem: Your edits are on disk but not yet selected for the next commit. Which area currently holds them?
questions.explanation: Unstaged edits live in the workspace on disk. They only enter the index after 'add', the local repo after 'commit', and the remote after 'push'. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: Which statement about 'git clone' is FALSE?

options.text: It creates a complete local copy of the repository
It links the copy to the remote named origin
It creates a shallow copy with no history
Its argument can be a file, http, or ssh URL

questions.explanation: It is false that clone makes a history-less shallow copy; clone creates a complete local copy. It does link to origin and accepts file/http/ssh URLs. (Git Lab p.15)

questions.stem: Which command stages a changed file (workspace -> index)?
questions.explanation: git add moves changes from the workspace into the index/stage. commit records the index into the local repo, push uploads, and pull receives. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: A teammate insists 'in Git the server always has the latest because commit auto-syncs.' Which correction is right?

options.text: the server updates on every fetch by any client
the server updates whenever you run status
commit syncs to the server automatically
commit is local; the server only updates when someone pushes

questions.explanation: commit records locally; the server is only updated by a push. status is read-only and does not sync, and a fetch downloads from the server rather than updating it. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: A student says 'the V-model proves agile beat waterfall.' Why is this a category error?

options.text: The V-model and CHAOS data are the same slide
The V-model is the agile paradigm itself
The V-model is a life-span model, not a paradigm comparison; agile-vs-waterfall is shown by CHAOS data
The V-model is waterfall, so it disproves agile

questions.explanation: The V-model is a life-span model pairing design with testing, not a paradigm-success comparison; agile-vs-waterfall success is shown by the CHAOS resolution table. The other options conflate distinct concepts. (Introduction p.19, p.24)

questions.stem: What is the central flaw baked into waterfall's core assumption?

options.text: It assumes requirements are fixed up front
It assumes the team never understands the code
It assumes versions ship in parallel
It assumes every phase is automated

questions.explanation: Waterfall assumes requirements are fixed up front, which is its appeal and its flaw because real requirements change. Automation, parallel versions, and code understanding are not its defining assumption. (Introduction p.15)

questions.stem: Which single statement combines BOTH waterfall anomalies the deck presents?

options.text: Agile is legally mandated for all projects
Construction metaphors are forbidden in software
Requirements change continuously (~2-3%/month) and CHAOS data show high challenged/failed rates
Disk space runs out and developers cannot clone

questions.explanation: Both anomalies are requirements volatility (~2-3%/month) and the CHAOS challenged/failed rates. The other options are irrelevant distractors. (Introduction p.16-17)

questions.stem: What do software life-span models, as a group, claim to offer the software-engineering discipline?

options.text: A simplified, comprehensive view of the entire discipline
A guarantee that projects finish on budget
A ranking of VCS tools by speed
A precise estimate of monthly requirements change

questions.explanation: Life-span models offer a simplified and comprehensive view of the entire software-engineering discipline. They are not estimates of requirements change, VCS rankings, or budget guarantees. (Introduction p.28)

questions.stem: Which best describes why generated build output should be kept out of version control?

options.text: Git physically cannot store any binary file
Build output is encrypted and unreadable to Git
Committing build output deletes the commit history
Generated and large binary artifacts bloat history and conflict badly; only source should be committed

questions.explanation: The rule is commit source, not build output, because generated/large binary artifacts bloat history and conflict badly. Git can store binaries; it just should not for build output, and committing them does not delete history. (Git Lab p.6)

questions.stem: A developer says 'I did git pull, so my new feature is now on the remote for everyone.' What is wrong with this?

options.text: pull only brings remote changes to you; sharing your own work requires push
pull is the same as push
pull sends your work and receives theirs simultaneously
pull uploads your commits before merging

questions.explanation: pull = fetch + merge only brings remote changes into your workspace; it never uploads your own commits, which requires push. The other options confuse pull with push. (Git Lab p.11)

questions.stem: An exam answer defines a 'life-span model' as a list of Git commands. Which option instead states what a software life-span model actually describes?

options.text: The six reasons to use version control
The stages software goes through from conception to death
The four areas of a Git repository

questions.explanation: A life-span model describes the stages software passes through from conception to death. The other options describe Git areas, VCS benefits, and essential difficulties. (Introduction p.20)

questions.stem: In the day-one walkthrough, which step is the practical embodiment of 'Initiation', occurring before any code is touched?

options.text: Filing the change request as a GitHub Issue
Pushing the feature branch to origin

questions.explanation: Filing the change request as a GitHub Issue is the Initiation step, entering the backlog before any code is touched. Building, pushing, and launching the GUI all come after code work begins. (Git Lab p.19)

questions.stem: You only want to see which files you have modified, without seeing the line-by-line changes. Which command is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: git status lists which files you changed. git diff shows the line-by-line changes, git show displays the last commit, and git commit records changes. (Git Lab p.13)
